questions.question: Laser engravers and cutters work by doing what to material?
options: Freezing the surface to make it brittle for mechanical scoring
Focusing enough light energy onto a tiny area to vaporize or melt the material
Etching with UV-reactive photopolymer chemistry
Mechanically milling with a rotating bit guided by a laser sensor

questions.question: What is "projection mapping" in the context of large-scale art installation?
options: Scaling a digital design file to the physical size of the material
Projecting imagery precisely onto three-dimensional surfaces or objects to create an illusion
Mapping UV coordinates onto a 3D mesh before laser cutting
Calculating the material cost of a large artwork from a digital file

questions.question: For engraving a large solid filled area, a laser typically uses "raster" mode. Why is vector mode less efficient for fills?
options: Vector paths are limited to 256 nodes by laser firmware
Vector tracing an outline can only produce a border — it can't fill an area without thousands of offset paths
Vector mode is only FDA-certified for cutting, not engraving
Vector mode requires reflective mirrors not present in CO₂ laser systems

talks.title: Hector Berlioz Was a Visionary Composer and also a Creepy Stalker
talks.speaker: Matt Browne

questions.question: Which symphony did Hector Berlioz compose that was directly inspired by his obsessive infatuation with an actress?
options: Symphony No. 9 in D minor
Symphonie Fantastique
The Unfinished Symphony
Eroica Symphony

questions.question: Who was the actress Berlioz became obsessed with after seeing her perform Shakespeare in Paris in 1827?
options: Sarah Bernhardt
Mrs. Patrick Campbell
Harriet Smithson
Ellen Terry

questions.question: What musical device did Berlioz use in Symphonie Fantastique to represent his beloved throughout all five movements?
options: A leitmotif — a short recurring theme
An idée fixe ("fixed idea") — a longer recurring melody that transforms with each movement
A cantus firmus — a slow-moving theme in the bass
A passacaglia — a theme repeated over a recurring bass progression

originStory.text:
Hi. I'm Justin Fritz — and I'm a Nerd Nite Boss.

That's a real title. The Nerd Nite organization gave it to me, and I take it very seriously.

Nerd Nite started in Boston in 2003. Graduate student Chris Balakrishnan had been telling the same stories at his local bar — The Midway Cafe — about studying parasitic birds in Cameroon. The bartenders finally told him: just give a talk. One night. Slides. Get it out of your system.

He did. It worked.

In 2006, his roommate Matt Wasowski moved the concept to New York City, expanded it beyond science to any topic worth a drink and 20 minutes, and built it into a global brand — the "Discovery Channel with beer" — now in over 100 cities worldwide.

It came to Fort Collins because of me and one other very important person: Senne Van Loon.

But first — trivia.

Senne and I built this thing from scratch.

Senne is the kind of person who hears "let's put on a nerdy event at a bar" and says yes immediately — full commitment, no hesitation. That energy is rare and it matters. You cannot build something like this alone.

As we grew, Jamie Fritz joined us. Jamie is the backbone — always behind the scenes, making everything work, never needing the spotlight. The kind of person every organization needs and rarely thanks enough.

Jamie has been onstage exactly once: to introduce Marybeth Arcodia. Which brings me to Marybeth.

Marybeth Arcodia was our host.

She was brilliant at it — warm, sharp, funny in exactly the right way for a room full of nerds on their third drink. She held the stage like she was born for it.

Marybeth eventually left Fort Collins for a faculty position in Miami. We were sad to lose her and enormously proud of her.

Hannah Bauer stepped up as host after Marybeth — and she's been incredible.

Here's the thing you need to understand about Nerd Nite: once you're a Boss, you're always a Boss. Marybeth is still a Boss. Distance doesn't change that.

You just played Nerdometer.

Every edition is built around a real Nerd Nite Fort Collins event — the actual talks, the actual speakers, the real nerdy obsessions people bring to a bar and share with strangers.

Senne. Jamie. Marybeth. Hannah. Every speaker who ever trusted a room full of people with the thing they love most.

All Bosses. Always.

Come be part of it.

— Justin Fritz, Nerd Nite Fort Collins Boss
